act: Narcotic Drugs and Psychotropic Substances Act 1985
description: Law governing narcotic drugs, psychotropic substances, and the penalties for their production, possession, sale, and consumption in India.

sections.title: Punishment for Cannabis/Ganja
sections.text: Whoever contravenes provisions relating to cannabis plant and cannabis: Small quantity — rigorous imprisonment up to 1 year, fine up to Rs. 10,000; Quantity greater than small but less than commercial — up to 10 years and fine up to Rs. 1 lakh; Commercial quantity — minimum 10 years up to 20 years, fine Rs. 1 to 2 lakh.
sections.simplified: Possession of ganja/cannabis: Small amount — up to 1 year jail and Rs. 10,000 fine. Medium amount — up to 10 years and Rs. 1 lakh fine. Large (commercial) amount — 10 to 20 years and Rs. 1-2 lakh fine.
sections.punishment: Small: 1 year, Rs. 10,000; Medium: 10 years, Rs. 1 lakh; Commercial: 10-20 years, Rs. 1-2 lakh

sections.title: Punishment for Manufactured Drugs
sections.text: Whoever contravenes provisions relating to manufactured drugs and prepared opium: Small quantity — rigorous imprisonment up to 1 year, fine up to Rs. 10,000; Quantity greater than small but less than commercial — up to 10 years and fine up to Rs. 1 lakh; Commercial quantity — minimum 10 years up to 20 years, fine Rs. 1 to 2 lakh.
sections.simplified: For manufactured drugs (heroin, cocaine, etc.): punishments follow the same structure as cannabis but quantities are much smaller. Even a few grams of hard drugs can attract 10-20 years imprisonment.
sections.punishment: Small: 1 year; Medium: 10 years; Commercial: 10-20 years

sections.title: Consumption of Narcotic Drugs
sections.text: Whoever consumes any narcotic drug or psychotropic substance shall be punishable: for cocaine, morphine, heroin — rigorous imprisonment up to 1 year and fine up to Rs. 20,000; for other drugs — imprisonment up to 6 months and fine up to Rs. 10,000. The court may release the addict for treatment.
sections.simplified: Using drugs personally is also a crime, but with lighter punishment: up to 1 year for hard drugs, up to 6 months for others. However, the court can send you for de-addiction treatment instead of jail.
sections.punishment: Hard drugs: 1 year; Others: 6 months. Court may order treatment instead

sections.title: Bail Restrictions for Drug Offences
sections.text: Notwithstanding anything contained in the Code of Criminal Procedure, no person accused of an offence punishable for offences involving commercial quantity shall be released on bail unless the Public Prosecutor has been given opportunity to oppose the application and the court is satisfied that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the accused is not guilty.
sections.simplified: Getting bail in NDPS cases involving commercial quantities is very difficult. The court CANNOT grant bail unless it believes you are likely not guilty. This is much stricter than normal bail provisions.

sections.title: Offences to be Cognizable and Non-Bailable
sections.text: Every offence punishable under this Act shall be cognizable and non-bailable.
sections.simplified: All drug offences under NDPS are cognizable (police can arrest without warrant) and non-bailable (bail is not a right, court decides). This makes drug charges very serious.

sections.title: Disposal of Seized Drugs
sections.text: The Magistrate shall draw samples and certify, photograph, and weigh the seized drugs in the presence of the person from whose custody the drugs were seized. The remaining drugs may then be disposed of.
sections.simplified: When police seize drugs, they must: photograph, weigh, and draw samples in front of the accused and a Magistrate. This procedure must be correctly followed — if not, it can be grounds for getting the case dismissed.
